did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning and AI methods for skin cancer diagnosis. Specifically, it employs the Segment Anything Model (SAM) for lesion segmentation and StyleGAN2-ADA for synthetic data generation to address class imbalance in the ISIC 2019 dataset. It then trains and evaluates eight pre-trained deep learning models (including CNNs like Xception and ViT) for skin lesion classification.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work, including a proposed augmentation pipeline using SAM and StyleGAN2-ADA to address class imbalance in the ISIC 2019 dataset. It details the methodology for synthesizing balanced datasets, training and evaluating eight pre-trained deep learning models (CNNs and ViTs), and reports specific quantitative results (e.g., 66% test accuracy, macro F1-score of 0.64). Although published in a conference proceedings (ICABME), it contains original research results rather than being a narrative or systematic review.

title: SAM-GAN Synthesis for Equitable Skin Cancer Diagnosis: A Deep Learning Approach to Address Class Imbalance in Dermoscopy
journal: 2025 Eighth International Conference on Advances in Biomedical Engineering (ICABME)
brief_summary: This study addresses severe class imbalance in the ISIC 2019 skin cancer classification task by proposing a synthetic data augmentation pipeline using the Segment Anything Model (SAM) and StyleGAN2-ADA. The method generates two fully balanced datasets containing 2,400 and 7,200 images per class to enhance representation of underrepresented lesion types like Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesions, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma. Eight pre-trained deep learning models, including CNNs and Vision Transformers, were trained and evaluated on these balanced datasets. The Xception model achieved the best generalization performance on the 7.2k dataset with a test accuracy of 66% and a macro F1-score of 0.64. Although synthetic fidelity challenges persisted, the framework demonstrates a viable strategy for equitable skin cancer diagnosis under data constraints.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the eight diagnostic classes in the ISIC 2019 dataset. Used in classification experiments. In the 7.2k balanced dataset, it had >4500 original samples and an FID score of 27.
The most over-represented class in ISIC 2019 (12,875 images). Downsampled to 2,400 and 7,200 images for balanced datasets. FID score of 26 in the 7.2k dataset.

One of the eight diagnostic classes in ISIC 2019. Augmented using geometric transformations only (no SAM/GAN synthesis due to clear boundaries not being the primary focus or sufficient original samples). FID score of 44 in the 7.2k dataset.

An underrepresented class in ISIC 2019 (239 images). Specifically targeted for SAM-based segmentation and StyleGAN2-ADA synthesis to address class imbalance. FID score of 58 in the 7.2k dataset.

all_task_types.target: Eight diagnostic skin lesion classes (Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevi, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratoses, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesions, Squamous Cell Carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification of dermoscopic images into discrete diagnostic categories using eight pre-trained deep learning models (CNNs and ViTs) trained on balanced synthetic datasets. Performance evaluated via accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

all_task_types.target: Synthetic dermoscopic images of skin lesions
all_task_types.details: Image generation using StyleGAN2-ADA to create balanced datasets (2.4k and 7.2k images per class) for data augmentation, addressing class imbalance in the ISIC 2019 dataset.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019 Dataset
datasets_used.description: The primary public dataset used in the study, containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions. It is heavily skewed with Melanocytic Nevi (NV) dominating the samples. The dataset includes a predefined test set for unbiased evaluation.

use.training: Used as the source for creating balanced synthetic datasets (2.4k and 7.2k per class) via SAM-based segmentation and StyleGAN2-ADA synthesis. For high-sample classes (MEL, BCC, BKL), GANs were trained directly on original images. For NV, it was downsampled. For underrepresented classes (DF, VASC, SCC), SAM segmentation and background synthesis were applied.
use.validation: A 10% split of the synthetic balanced datasets was used for validation during model fine-tuning.
use.testing: The official predefined ISIC 2019 test set was kept untouched and used for final evaluation of the trained models to ensure unbiased assessment.
use.other: Used for exploratory data analysis to identify class imbalance and lesion morphology characteristics.

datasets_used.name: Synthetic 2.4k Balanced Dataset
datasets_used.description: A fully balanced dataset created by the authors where all eight classes were equalized to 2,400 images each. It was generated using SAM-based segmentation, StyleGAN2-ADA synthesis, and geometric transformations.

use.training: Used to train eight pre-trained deep learning models (VGG16/19, EfficientNetV2-B0, DenseNet-201, Xception, InceptionResNetV2, ConvNeXt-Base/Large, ViT) for classification.
use.validation: 10% of this dataset was used for validation during the training process.
use.testing: Not used for testing; the official ISIC 2019 test set was used instead.
use.other: Used to benchmark model performance under severe class imbalance conditions compared to the 7.2k dataset.

datasets_used.name: Synthetic 7.2k Balanced Dataset
datasets_used.description: A fully balanced dataset created by the authors where all eight classes were expanded to 7,200 images each. It was generated using SAM-based segmentation, StyleGAN2-ADA synthesis, and geometric transformations.

use.training: Used to train eight pre-trained deep learning models (VGG16/19, EfficientNetV2-B0, DenseNet-201, Xception, InceptionResNetV2, ConvNeXt-Base/Large, ViT) for classification.
use.validation: 10% of this dataset was used for validation during the training process.
use.testing: Not used for testing; the official ISIC 2019 test set was used instead.
use.other: Used to benchmark model performance with increased data volume and to evaluate the impact of synthetic augmentation on generalization.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the realism and quality of synthetic images generated by StyleGAN2-ADA, serving as a metric for generative performance and convergence.

To provide class-wise comparison of actual vs. predicted labels using True Positives (TP), True Negatives (TN), False Positives (FP), and False Negatives (FN) to evaluate per-class performance and misclassifications.
To summarize model performance metrics for each class, including weighted and macro averages, to assess generalization and fairness across imbalanced classes.
To assess model interpretability by highlighting influential regions in input images that contributed most to the output class predictions.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported in the paper.

Synthetic 2.4k Balanced Dataset.metric: Accuracy (Training/Validation/Test), Precision/Macro Precision, Macro Recall, Macro F1-Score
Synthetic 2.4k Balanced Dataset.notes: All models achieved high training and validation accuracies (≥84%), but showed a consistent generalization gap with test accuracies ranging between 32% and 50%. Xception achieved the best balance with 50% test accuracy and a weighted F1-score of 0.47. ViT performed the worst with 32% accuracy and an F1-score of 0.27.

StyleGAN2-ADA Synthesis Quality (7.2k Da.notes: FID scores indicate image quality. Lower scores are better. Classes with abundant samples (BCC, MEL) had lower FID scores (26, 27). Underrepresented classes relying on SAM segmentation (VASC, DF) had higher FID scores (62, 58), reflecting generation challenges.

architecture_used.main_model: Eight pre-trained deep learning models: VGG16, VGG19, EfficientNetV2-B0, DenseNet-201, Xception, InceptionResNetV2, ConvNeXt-Base, ConvNeXt-Large, and Vision Transformer (ViT). Additionally, StyleGAN2-ADA was used for image synthesis.
architecture_used.pretraining: All classification models were initialized with ImageNet weights.
architecture_used.input_size: Original ISIC 2019 images were resized from 1024x1024 to match the input dimensions required by each specific model.

training_details.data_split: 90% training, 10% validation (from synthetic datasets); official ISIC 2019 test set kept untouched for final evaluation.

preprocessing_techniques: Image Resizing: All ISIC 2019 images were resized from original 1024x1024 resolution to match input dimensions required by each specific deep learning model.

SAM-based Segmentation: The Segment Anything Model (SAM) was used to isolate lesion regions for underrepresented classes (Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesions, Squamous Cell Carcinoma) using manual prompting modes (click, box, everything).
Background Synthesis/Blending: Segmented lesions were composited onto synthetic skin-tone backgrounds (Fitzpatrick types IV–VI) and real dermoscopic skin to preserve contextual realism.
Geometric Transformations: Applied to augmented images to enhance diversity; specifically, horizontal and vertical flipping was used for Actinic Keratoses due to unclear boundaries.
Class Downsampling: The over-represented Melanocytic Nevi (NV) class was downsampled to 2,400 and 7,200 images to align with other classes and prevent model bias.
Data Splitting: Synthetic balanced datasets were split into 90% training and 10% validation; the official ISIC 2019 test set was kept untouched for final evaluation.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used for underrepresented classes (Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesions, Squamous Cell Carcinoma) via manual prompting modes (click, box, everything) to isolate lesion regions.

Used for synthetic image generation to balance datasets. Separate models trained per class using real ISIC 2019 images and SAM-generated composites. Initialized with pre-trained FFHQ model. Trained until Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) stabilized. Generated 7,200 images per class for the 7.2k dataset.
Segmented lesions composited onto synthetic skin-tone backgrounds (Fitzpatrick types IV–VI) and real dermoscopic skin to preserve contextual realism.
Applied to augmented images. Specifically, horizontal and vertical flipping used for Actinic Keratoses due to unclear boundaries.
Melanocytic Nevi (NV) class downsampled to 2,400 and 7,200 images to align with other classes and prevent model bias.
For high-sample classes (Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis), StyleGAN2-ADA was trained directly on original images without segmentation or background synthesis.

conclusions: The proposed augmentation pipeline using SAM-based segmentation and StyleGAN2-ADA synthesis successfully created fully balanced datasets (2.4k and 7.2k images per class) to address severe class imbalance in the ISIC 2019 dataset.

The pipeline enhanced representation of underrepresented classes (Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesions, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma) while preserving contextual realism through background blending and geometric transformations.

Among eight pre-trained deep learning models (CNNs and ViT), Xception trained on the 7.2k balanced dataset achieved the best generalization performance with 66% test accuracy and a macro F1-score of 0.64 (noted as 0.48 in detailed results tables).
Increasing the dataset size from 2.4k to 7.2k images per class improved classification performance across all evaluated models.
Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) outperformed Vision Transformers (ViT), which were hampered by limited data availability.

Synthetic fidelity challenges persisted for minority classes, evidenced by higher Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) scores for Vascular Lesions (62) and Dermatofibroma (58) compared to classes with abundant samples like Basal Cell Carcinoma (26) and Melanoma (27).
The framework offers a reproducible, end-to-end strategy for scalable and equitable skin lesion classification under real-world data constraints.
important_remarks: Significant discrepancy in reported performance: The Abstract and Conclusion claim a macro F1-score of 0.64 for Xception on the 7.2k dataset, whereas Table X (detailed results) reports a macro F1-score of 0.45 for the same model and dataset.
Limited external validation: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC 2019 dataset; no external or independent test sets were used to validate generalizability beyond the predefined ISIC test split.

Synthetic fidelity limitations: High Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) scores for underrepresented classes indicate generation challenges, specifically Vascular Lesions (FID 62) and Dermatofibroma (FID 58), compared to well-represented classes like Basal Cell Carcinoma (FID 26).

Poor Vision Transformer performance: ViT models exhibited the lowest generalization capabilities among tested architectures, with test accuracies as low as 32% on the 2.4k dataset and 54% on the 7.2k dataset, suggesting sensitivity to data volume or synthetic artifacts.

Methodological constraints on augmentation: Actinic Keratoses (AK) were augmented using only geometric transformations due to unclear lesion boundaries, rather than the full SAM-GAN pipeline, creating an inconsistency in the augmentation strategy across classes.

Potential data leakage risk: The synthetic datasets were split into 90% training and 10% validation for model tuning, while the official ISIC 2019 test set was kept untouched; however, the reliance on synthetic data for training raises concerns about overfitting to generated features rather than real-world pathology.

Conference publication status: The work was published in a conference proceedings (ICABME) rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may imply less rigorous scrutiny compared to journal publications.
